questionText: The product of all prime numbers between 2 and 99 (inclusive) ends in how many zeros?

steps.explanation: Count multiples of 2 among prime numbers from 2 to 99
steps.calculation: Only one prime is a multiple of 2, which is 2 itself

steps.explanation: Count multiples of 5 among prime numbers from 2 to 99
steps.calculation: Only one prime is a multiple of 5, which is 5 itself

steps.explanation: Number of trailing zeros = min(count of 2s, count of 5s) in the product

solution.shortcut: Trailing zeros = number of (2,5) pairs. In the list of primes, there is exactly one 2 and one 5.
solution.commonMistake: Counting all multiples of 2 and 5, not just the primes themselves.

questionText: Find the number of zeros at the end of the product $N = 3 \times 6 \times 9 \times 12 \times \ldots \times 102$.

steps.explanation: Express N in a simpler form
steps.calculation: Factor out 3 from each term: N = 3^{34} × 34!

steps.explanation: The highest power of 2 in 34! is much greater than that of 5, so zeros = highest power of 5 in 34!
Note: 3^34 contributes no factors of 2 or 5. The answer from solution is 6 based on the highest power of 5 in 34!
steps.calculation: The answer given is 6 (option c); the solution states highest power of 5 in 34! with computed value shown as 6

solution.shortcut: Write N = 3^34 × 34!, then count powers of 5 in 34!.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to factor out 3 from each term before applying Legendre's formula.

questionText: Find the unit digit in $(13)^{2003}$.

steps.explanation: Unit digit of powers of 3 follows a cycle of 4: 3, 9, 7, 1
3^4 gives unit digit 1, so (3^4)^500 gives unit digit 1
Unit digit of 3^2003 = unit digit of 3^3 = 7

solution.shortcut: Find remainder when exponent is divided by 4; use that power of the unit digit.
solution.commonMistake: Using the full number 13 instead of just its unit digit 3.

questionText: Find the unit digit in $3^{99}$.

steps.explanation: 3^4 gives unit digit 1, so (3^4)^24 gives unit digit 1

solution.shortcut: Divide exponent by 4; remainder 3 → unit digit 7.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing the remainder with the actual power.

questionText: Match the unit digits: (A) $(1827)^{16}$, (B) $(2153)^{19}$, (C) $(5129)^{21}$. Which of the following correctly maps each expression to its unit digit?

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (1827)^16: unit digit of 7^16. 7^4 gives unit digit 1, (7^4)^4 gives unit digit 1. So A → 1.
Unit digit of (2153)^19: unit digit of 3^19. 3^4 gives unit digit 1, 3^16 gives unit digit 1. 3^19 = 3^16 × 3^3 → unit digit = 1 × 7 = 7. So B → 7 (option label 4 in original).
Unit digit of (5129)^21: unit digit of 9^21. 9^2 gives unit digit 1, 9^20 gives unit digit 1. 9^21 = 9^20 × 9 → unit digit = 9. So C → 9 (option label 5 in original).

solution.shortcut: Identify the unit digit of the base, find the cycle length, use remainder of exponent ÷ cycle length.
solution.commonMistake: Not recognising that 9^even = unit digit 1 and 9^odd = unit digit 9.

questionText: Find the unit digit of $(67)^{25} - 1$.

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (67)^25 = unit digit of 7^25. 7^4 gives unit digit 1.
steps.calculation: 25 = 4×6 + 1, so unit digit of 7^25 = unit digit of 7^1 = 7

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (67^25 – 1) = unit digit of (7 – 1) = 6

solution.shortcut: Find unit digit of 7^25 first (= 7), then subtract 1.
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to subtract 1 at the end.

questionText: Find the unit digit in the product $86 \times 36 \times 35 \times 47 \times 13$... (a product ending in digits 4, 6, 5, 7, 3 in some arrangement). The unit digit of the product $4 \times 8 \times 7 \times 3$ is required.

steps.explanation: Only unit digits matter for the unit digit of the product
steps.calculation: 4 × 8 = 32, unit digit 2; 2 × 7 = 14, unit digit 4; 4 × 3 = 12, unit digit 2

steps.explanation: Unit digit of the full product is 2

solution.shortcut: Multiply unit digits only: 4×8×7×3 = 672, unit digit = 2.
solution.commonMistake: Multiplying all digits instead of only unit digits.

questionText: Find the value of $x$ if the unit digit in the product $9 \times 6 \times x \times 4$ is 8.

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (9 × 6 × x × 4) must equal 8
6 × x has unit digit 8 ⟹ x = 3 (since 6×3=18)

solution.shortcut: Work backwards: multiply known unit digits first, then determine missing digit.
solution.commonMistake: Trying to multiply all four at once without reducing step by step.

questionText: Find the unit digit in $7^{95} - 3^{58}$.

steps.explanation: Unit digit of 7^95: 7^4 gives unit digit 1. 95 = 4×23 + 3, so unit digit = unit digit of 7^3 = 3
Unit digit of 3^58: 3^4 gives unit digit 1. 58 = 4×14 + 2, so unit digit = unit digit of 3^2 = 9
Unit digit of (7^95 – 3^58): since 3 < 9, borrow: 13 – 9 = 4

solution.shortcut: Compute unit digits separately, then subtract (borrow if needed).
solution.commonMistake: Forgetting to borrow when the subtracted unit digit is larger.

questionText: Find the unit digit in $(784)^{126} + (784)^{127}$.

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (784)^126 = unit digit of 4^126. 4^2 = 16, unit digit 6; so any even power of 4 has unit digit 6.
steps.calculation: 126 is even, so unit digit of 4^126 = 6

steps.explanation: Unit digit of (784)^127 = unit digit of 4^127. 127 is odd, so unit digit of 4^127 = 4.
Unit digit of sum = unit digit of (6 + 4) = unit digit of 10 = 0

solution.shortcut: Powers of 4: even exponent → unit digit 6, odd exponent → unit digit 4. Sum = 10, unit digit = 0.
solution.commonMistake: Not distinguishing even vs odd exponents for base 4.

formulas.title: Unit digit of powers of 4
formulas.expression: 4^n \equiv 6 \pmod{10} \text{ if } n \text{ even};\quad 4^n \equiv 4 \pmod{10} \text{ if } n \text{ odd}

solution.shortcut: Replace each term by its unit digit, then compute the algebraic sum.
solution.commonMistake: Not noting that 1^n = 1, 5^n = 5, 6^n = 6 for all n ≥ 1.

questionText: Find the unit digit in the product $(41793) \times (5317) \times (1491)$... (a product of numbers with given unit digits 3, 7, 1 after simplification from 4^1793 × 5317 × 1491).

steps.explanation: Unit digit of 4^1793: 4^even → 6, 4^odd → 4. 1793 is odd, unit digit = 4
Unit digit of 5317 is 5 (any power of 5 ends in 5)
Unit digit of 1491 is 1 (any power of 1 ends in 1)
Unit digit of full product = unit digit of 4 × 5 × 1 = 20, which is 0

solution.shortcut: Any product containing a factor of 5 and an even number will end in 0.
solution.commonMistake: Calculating unit digit of 4^1793 incorrectly by not checking parity of exponent.

questionText: If $x = 2y$, what is the unit digit of $x^{4n}$ for any positive integer $n$?

steps.explanation: For y = 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9: unit digit of (16y^4)^n is 6
steps.calculation: 16y^4 has unit digit 6 for most y

steps.explanation: Hence unit digit of x^{4n} is 0 or 6

solution.shortcut: Check y=5 separately for unit digit 0; all others give unit digit 6.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming only one possible unit digit without checking y=5.

questionText: What is the unit digit in $(5^n + 6^m)$ for any positive integers $n$ and $m$?

steps.explanation: 5^n always ends in 5 for any positive integer n
6^m always ends in 6 for any positive integer m
Unit digit of (5^n + 6^m) = unit digit of (5 + 6) = unit digit of 11 = 1

solution.shortcut: 5^n always ends in 5, 6^m always ends in 6; sum ends in 1.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing 5^n with numbers like 15 or 25 which also end in 5.

questionText: What are the last two digits of $(2^{12n} - 6^{4n})$ for positive integer $n$?

steps.explanation: The last two digits are 00 for n=1; this generalises.

solution.shortcut: Substitute n=1 to find the pattern for last two digits.
solution.commonMistake: Not factoring out common powers of 2 before evaluating.

questionText: What is the last digit of $(0.2)^{2000}$?

steps.explanation: The last digit of (0.2)^{2000} equals the last digit of (0.2)^4 because 2000 is a multiple of 4
(0.2)^4 = (2/10)^4 = 16/10000 = 0.0016; the last non-zero digit pattern follows powers of 2: unit digit of 2^4 = 16 → 6

solution.shortcut: Unit digit of 2^{4k} is always 6.
solution.commonMistake: Treating 0.2 as 2 without considering decimal context.

questionText: How many digits does the product of two numbers having 19 and 15 digits respectively contain?

steps.explanation: When multiplying two numbers with x and y digits, the product has either (x+y-1) or (x+y) digits
Use sample: 36 × 34 = 1224 (2-digit × 2-digit → 4-digit product), confirming product can reach x+y digits
steps.calculation: Analogously, the product of a 19-digit and 15-digit number has 34 digits

solution.shortcut: Check whether leading digits of both numbers produce a carry; typically x+y digits result.
solution.commonMistake: Always assuming the product has exactly x+y digits without verification.

questionText: A number $\overline{xyz}$ is a multiple of 11. Which of the following must be true?

steps.explanation: Divisibility rule for 11: (sum of digits at odd places) – (sum of digits at even places) = 0 or multiple of 11
steps.calculation: For a 3-digit number xyz: odd places = z and x, even place = y

steps.explanation: So y = z + x

solution.shortcut: 11-divisibility for 3-digit number: middle digit = sum of outer digits.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing which positions are 'odd' and 'even'.

questionText: What is the least value of $n$ such that the number $6135n2$ is divisible by 9?

steps.explanation: Sum of digits of 6135n2 = 6+1+3+5+n+2 = 17+n
For divisibility by 9, (17+n) must be divisible by 9. Smallest n ≥ 0: 17+1=18 which is divisible by 9.

solution.shortcut: Find what value of n makes digit sum a multiple of 9.
solution.commonMistake: Checking divisibility by 3 instead of 9.

questionText: Which of the following is completely divisible by 11? Consider the number 978626.

steps.explanation: Apply divisibility rule for 11 to 978626
steps.calculation: Odd places (from right): 6, 6, 7 → sum = 19; Even places: 2, 8, 9 → sum = 19; Difference = 19 - 19 = 0

steps.explanation: Since difference = 0, 978626 is divisible by 11

solution.shortcut: Alternating digit sum = 0 ⟹ divisible by 11.
solution.commonMistake: Using digit sum instead of alternating digit sum.

questionText: The number 111111111111 (twelve 1s) is divisible by which of the following? (i) 3  (ii) 11  (iii) 37  (iv) 111

steps.explanation: Sum of digits = 12, divisible by 3. So divisible by 3.
Alternating sum: (6 ones at odd places) – (6 ones at even places) = 6 – 6 = 0. Divisible by 11.

solution.shortcut: Repunit (all-1s number) is divisible by 3, 11, 37, 111, and 1001.
solution.commonMistake: Checking only one or two divisors.

questionText: Is 65043 divisible by 18?
options: D) No, not divisible by either 2 or 9
correctAnswer: D) No, not divisible by either 2 or 9

steps.explanation: 18 = 2 × 9, where 2 and 9 are co-primes. For divisibility by 18, the number must be divisible by both 2 and 9.
65043 ends in 3 (odd), so it is not divisible by 2.
Since 65043 is not divisible by 2, it is not divisible by 18.

solution.shortcut: Check divisibility by 2 first (look at last digit). Odd number ⟹ not divisible by 18.
solution.commonMistake: Only checking one factor of 18 (e.g., just 9).

formulas.title: Divisibility by product of coprimes
formulas.expression: a \mid n \text{ and } b \mid n \Leftrightarrow ab \mid n, \text{ when } \gcd(a,b)=1

questionText: In the number $8\square0$, if the digit at the blank is $x$, find $x$ such that the number is divisible by 4.

steps.explanation: A number is divisible by 4 if its last two digits form a number divisible by 4.
80 + x must be divisible by 4. Check: 80 is divisible by 4. So x must also make (80+x) divisible by 4, meaning x must be divisible by 4.
steps.calculation: x = 0 or 4 or 8... but solution says x = 4

solution.shortcut: Check last two digits for divisibility by 4; 80+4 = 84 = 4×21.
solution.commonMistake: Checking the entire number instead of just the last two digits.

questionText: Is 4006020 divisible by 3?

steps.explanation: 12 is divisible by 3, so 4006020 is divisible by 3

solution.shortcut: Sum digits; if divisible by 3, the number is too.
solution.commonMistake: Including non-digit characters or miscalculating digit sum.

questionText: Which of the following statements is true? (a) Every integer is a whole number. (b) Every rational number is an integer. (c) Every integer is a rational number. (d) Every rational number is a real number.

steps.explanation: Every integer is a rational number (p/q form with q=1), and every rational number is a real number. However, not every integer is a whole number (negative integers exist), and not every rational number is an integer.
steps.calculation: Statement (d): every rational number is a real number — TRUE

solution.shortcut: Number hierarchy: Natural ⊂ Whole ⊂ Integer ⊂ Rational ⊂ Real.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing subset relationships in the number system hierarchy.

questionText: Which of the following numbers is exactly divisible by 11? (a) 4823718  (b) 4832718  (c) 4823178  (d) 4382718

solution.shortcut: Apply alternating digit sum rule to each option until one gives a multiple of 11.
solution.commonMistake: Applying divisibility rule for 3 instead of 11.

questionText: Is 17325 divisible by 15?

steps.explanation: Unit digit of 17325 is 5. So it is divisible by 5.
Sum of digits = 5+2+3+7+1 = 18, divisible by 3. So divisible by 3.
Since 5 and 3 are co-primes and 17325 is divisible by both, it is divisible by 15.

solution.shortcut: 15 = 3 × 5. Check divisibility by 3 and 5 separately.
solution.commonMistake: Only checking one of the factors (3 or 5).

questionText: Which of the following is true about 7386038? (i) Divisible by 3  (ii) Divisible by 9  (iii) Divisible by 4  (iv) Divisible by 11

steps.explanation: Sum of digits = 7+3+8+6+0+3+8 = 35. Not divisible by 3 or 9.
steps.calculation: 35 ÷ 3 is not integer; 35 ÷ 9 is not integer

steps.explanation: Last two digits = 38. 38 ÷ 4 = 9.5, not an integer. Not divisible by 4.
Alternating digit sum: (8+0+8+7) – (3+6+3) = 23 – 12 = 11, divisible by 11.

solution.shortcut: Check digit sum for 3/9, last two digits for 4, alternating sum for 11.
solution.commonMistake: Not verifying each divisibility condition independently.

formulas.title: Divisibility rules summary
formulas.expression: \text{3: digit sum; 4: last 2 digits; 9: digit sum; 11: alternating sum}

questionText: Each of the numbers 24984, 26784, and 28584 is divisible by which of the following? (i) 3  (ii) 9  (iii) 4

steps.explanation: Last two digits: 84. 84 ÷ 4 = 21. Divisible by 4.
All three numbers are divisible by 3, 9, and 4.

solution.shortcut: All three numbers have the same digit sum (27) and the same last two digits (84).
solution.commonMistake: Checking only one number instead of all three.

questionText: Is 923872 divisible by 8?

steps.explanation: A number is divisible by 8 if its last three digits form a number divisible by 8.

solution.shortcut: Check last 3 digits: 872 ÷ 8 = 109, remainder 0.
solution.commonMistake: Only checking last 2 digits for divisibility by 8 (rule requires last 3).

questionText: In the number $549x38y$, if the number is divisible by 11, find $x$ given the expression $(5+9+x+7) - (4+3+8) = 6+x$ implies $x=5$.

steps.explanation: Apply the divisibility rule for 11 to the number 549x38y (reading: 5,4,9,x,3,8,y with certain digit positions)
steps.calculation: For divisibility by 11, this must equal 0 or 11

solution.shortcut: Set up alternating digit sum equation and solve for x.
solution.commonMistake: Using wrong place values in the alternating sum.

questionText: If each of $m$ and $n$ is divisible by 5, which of the following is necessarily true?

steps.explanation: Take m=15, n=20. Both are divisible by 5.

35 is not divisible by 10. So '(m+n) is divisible by 10' is NOT necessarily true. The correct answer based on solution is (a) which the solution DISPROVES — so the answer must be re-read as option (a) being the statement that is NOT true.
The solution shows that (m+n) is divisible by 10 is NOT always true, hence the answer is (a) — the statement that is false/not necessarily true.
steps.calculation: Answer: (m+n) divisible by 10 is the false statement

solution.shortcut: Use counter-example: m=15, n=20; m+n=35, not divisible by 10.
solution.commonMistake: Assuming divisibility by 5 implies sum is divisible by 10.

formulas.title: Divisibility and sum
formulas.expression: 5 \mid m \text{ and } 5 \mid n \Rightarrow 5 \mid (m+n), \text{ but not necessarily } 10 \mid (m+n)

questionText: An integer is divisible by 16 if and only if which of the following holds?
options: A) The last two digits form a number divisible by 16
B) The number formed by the last 4 digits is divisible by 16
C) The digit sum is divisible by 16
D) The last three digits form a number divisible by 16
correctAnswer: B) The number formed by the last 4 digits is divisible by 16

steps.explanation: 16 = 2^4. The divisibility rule for 2^k is: a number is divisible by 2^k if and only if its last k digits form a number divisible by 2^k.

solution.shortcut: 16 = 2^4, so check last 4 digits.
solution.commonMistake: Using last 2 or last 3 digits instead of last 4.

questionText: Which of the following is divisible by each of 3, 7, 9, and 11? (a) 639  (b) 2079  (c) 3321  (d) 4563

steps.explanation: Check 639: not divisible by 7 (639 = 7×91 + 2).
Check 2079: digit sum = 2+0+7+9 = 18, divisible by 3 and 9.
2079 ÷ 7: using the rule 79 – 2 = 77 = 7×11. Divisible by 7.
2079 alternating sum: (9+0) – (7+2) = 9 – 9 = 0. Divisible by 11.
steps.result: divisible by 11, hence 2079 is the answer

solution.shortcut: Check each condition for 2079: digit sum 18 (3,9 ✓), alternating sum 0 (11 ✓), and test for 7.
solution.commonMistake: Skipping the divisibility check for 7.

formulas.title: Divisibility by 7 (shortcut)
formulas.expression: \text{Remove last digit, double it, subtract from remaining; repeat}

questionText: A six-digit number $476xy0$ is divisible by both 3 and 11. Find $x$ and $y$.

steps.explanation: For divisibility by 11: (0+x+7) – (y+6+4) = 0 ⟹ x – y – 3 = 0 ⟹ x – y = 3
For divisibility by 3: (4+7+6+x+y+0) = 17+x+y must be divisible by 3
steps.calculation: x+y must be 1, 4, 7, 10,... Since x≠0, y≠0, x+y ≠ 1

steps.explanation: Try x+y=4 with x-y=3: x=7/2 (not integer). Try x+y=7 with x-y=3: x=5, y=2.

solution.shortcut: Set up two equations (11-divisibility and 3-divisibility) and solve simultaneously.
solution.commonMistake: Not considering that x+y=4 leads to a non-integer solution.

questionText: How many of the following numbers are divisible by 3 but not by 9? 27, 45, 63, 18, 63, 45, 63, 63, 27, 72 (digit sums 9, 12, 18, 9, 21, 12, 18, 21, 15, 24)

steps.explanation: Compute digit sums of the given numbers: sums are 9, 12, 18, 9, 21, 12, 18, 21, 15, 24
Divisible by 9: digit sum divisible by 9 → sums 9, 18, 9, 18 → 4 numbers. Divisible by 3 but NOT 9: sums 12, 21, 12, 21, 15, 24 → 6 numbers.

solution.shortcut: Filter by digit sum: divisible by 3 but not by 9 means digit sum is multiple of 3 but not 9.
solution.commonMistake: Including numbers with digit sum divisible by 9.

questionText: The number $357y25x$ is divisible by both 3 and 5. What are the values of $x$ and $y$?

steps.explanation: Divisibility by 5: last digit x must be 0 or 5
Divisibility by 3: digit sum (22+x+y) must be divisible by 3, so x+y must satisfy (22+x+y) ≡ 0 (mod 3)
Taking x=0: y must satisfy 0+y ≡ 2 (mod 3), so y=2. Check: 357225x = 3572250, digit sum = 3+5+7+2+2+5+0=24, divisible by 3 ✓. Units digit 0 divisible by 5 ✓.

solution.shortcut: x=0 for divisibility by 5, then solve for y using digit sum condition.
solution.commonMistake: Not checking both x=0 and x=5 systematically.

questionText: 6897 is divisible by which of the following? (a) 11 only  (b) 19 only  (c) both 11 and 19  (d) neither 11 nor 19

steps.explanation: Check divisibility by 11: alternating sum (7+8)–(9+6) = 15–15 = 0. Divisible by 11.
Check divisibility by 19: 6897 ÷ 19 = 363. Divisible by 19.
6897 is divisible by both 11 and 19.

solution.shortcut: Check 11 by alternating digit sum, check 19 by direct division.
solution.commonMistake: Only checking one of the two divisors.

questionText: Which of the following numbers is divisible by 24? (a) 35718  (b) 63810  (c) 537804  (d) 3125736

steps.explanation: 24 = 3 × 8, where gcd(3,8)=1. Need divisibility by both 3 and 8.
steps.calculation: Check last 3 digits for 8, digit sum for 3

steps.explanation: 35718: last 3 digits = 718. 718 ÷ 8 = 89.75. Not divisible by 8.
63810: last 3 digits = 810. 810 ÷ 8 = 101.25. Not divisible by 8.
537804: last 3 digits = 804. 804 ÷ 8 = 100.5. Not divisible by 8.
3125736: last 3 digits = 736. 736 ÷ 8 = 92 ✓. Digit sum = 3+1+2+5+7+3+6=27, divisible by 3 ✓. Divisible by 24.

solution.shortcut: 24=3×8: last 3 digits must be divisible by 8, digit sum divisible by 3.
solution.commonMistake: Checking last 2 digits for 8 (need last 3).

questionText: A number consists of 21 threes and 14 ones. Is it divisible by 3, 11, both, or neither?

steps.explanation: Digit sum = 21×3 + 14×1 = 63 + 14 = 77. 77 ÷ 3 is not an integer (77 = 3×25 + 2). Not divisible by 3.
For divisibility by 11, compute alternating digit sum. From solution: (odd-place sum) – (even-place sum) = 3, which is neither 0 nor a multiple of 11. Not divisible by 11.
Hence divisible by neither 3 nor 11.

solution.shortcut: Compute total digit sum; check against 3. Use alternating sum for 11.
solution.commonMistake: Confusing digit sum with alternating digit sum.

questionText: The number 325325 is divisible by which of the following? (a) 7 only  (b) 11 only  (c) 13 only  (d) 7, 11, and 13

steps.explanation: 325325 = 325 × 1001. Note that 325 – 325 = 0, so it's divisible by 7 (property of 6-digit repunit-type).
Check 11: alternating sum (5+3+2) – (2+5+3) = 10 – 10 = 0. Divisible by 11.

solution.shortcut: Any 6-digit number of the form ABCABC = ABC × 1001 = ABC × 7 × 11 × 13.
solution.commonMistake: Not recognising the ABCABC = ABC×1001 pattern.

questionText: A number $30X0103$ is divisible by 13. Find the digit $X$.

steps.explanation: Divide the number into groups of 3 from the right: 3 | 0X0 | 103
For divisibility by 13 using the alternating sum of 3-digit groups: (103 + 3) – 0X0 must be divisible by 13
0X0 = 10X (the number with X in tens place). Try X=8: 080 = 80. 106–80 = 26 = 2×13 ✓

solution.shortcut: Use 3-digit group alternating sum rule for 13.
solution.commonMistake: Using single-digit alternating sum rule instead of 3-digit group method.

questionText: A number divisible by both 11 and 13 is necessarily divisible by which of the following?

steps.explanation: 11 and 13 are co-prime (gcd = 1). If a number is divisible by both, it is divisible by 11×13.

solution.shortcut: Co-prime divisors: if both divide n, their product also divides n.
solution.commonMistake: Adding instead of multiplying co-prime divisors.

formulas.title: Co-prime divisibility
formulas.expression: \gcd(a,b)=1,\ a \mid n,\ b \mid n \Rightarrow ab \mid n

questionText: Which of the following numbers is NOT divisible by 7? (i) 195195  (ii) 181181  (iii) 120120  (iv) 891891
options: D) None — all are divisible by 7
correctAnswer: D) None — all are divisible by 7

steps.explanation: All four numbers are of the form ABCABC = ABC × 1001, and 1001 = 7 × 11 × 13. So all are divisible by 7.

solution.shortcut: Any ABCABC number = ABC × 1001 = ABC × 7 × 11 × 13. Always divisible by 7.
solution.commonMistake: Performing long division instead of recognising the 1001 pattern.

questionText: If $653xy$ is divisible by 80, find $x + y$.

steps.explanation: 80 = 16 × 5. For divisibility by 5, last digit must be 0 or 5. For divisibility by 16, last 4 digits must be divisible by 16. Since divisible by both 5 and 16 (80=16×5), y=0.
Now 653x0 must be divisible by 16. The last 4 digits are 3x00... actually the last 4 digits of 653x0 are 53x0.

solution.shortcut: 80=16×5: y=0 (divisibility by 5), then find x such that last 4 digits divisible by 16.
solution.commonMistake: Using last 2 digits for divisibility by 16 instead of last 4.

formulas.title: Divisibility by 80
formulas.expression: 80 = 16 \times 5 \Rightarrow \text{check last digit for 5, last 4 digits for 16}
